The first thing that you're going to be, it's almost like the world will, and your psyche and your psychology will say, check your inbox, go check your email, check your email, check your social, check your inbox. And the only thing waiting in your inbox is the world's agenda for you, and you have to impose your own agenda on you, not let the world impose its agenda on you. So you have to protect your time. You have to be aware that there are time vampires out there. There are going to be expectations that people will have for you. They might be clients, they might be prospects, they might be vendors, they might be employees or team members. They are going to have this way that they think you should act, and that way will take time from you, and you have to stand firm. Being productive is not about getting a lot of things done. Being productive is not about working hard. You go to the bank and you go to deposit a check, and they don't ask you if you worked hard for it. So I grew up in the Midwest here in the United States, and there's a really strong work ethic in the Midwest. And so sometimes I get stuck in a slupe of thinking I have to work really, really hard, and I'm just going to outwork everyone. But that's not what being productive is. And really when we're talking about leverage, we're talking about making your time super productive. Being productive is about getting the right things done, doing the right things, knowing the things that are going to make an impact, not the things that other people think you should do. So be really careful, understand that there are time vampires. They're both norms, in other words, the things that people assume you should do.

## [03:04 - 06:04]

And then there are people that are time vampires that will just show up in your life and just drain time and drain energy from you. There are drama queens and drama kings, and they'll just create drama everywhere, and that drama will just suck the time and energy out of you. So you just have to refuse to play that game. You have to remove yourself from those situations and frankly from some of those relationships. And maybe some of those relationships are tough to remove yourselves from. Maybe it's your parents or your kids or something like that, and it makes it really hard. But you have to continually focus and stay away from those things that take you off your game. Because at the end of the day, you, well, we all have the same amount of time, right? And you're here listening to this because you want to create more. And again, it could be creating more income, create a better lifestyle, create more impact, help more people, serve more people. To do that, you have to guard your time carefully. You need to ask yourself, does this, whatever you're thinking about doing, if I take this on, if I do this, does this take more time from me? Does my time commitment grow as this thing grows, as this project grows? Can I get leverage here? Can I design it so that it grows without me putting in more time? Or is it inherently a situation where I'm going to continually have to put in more time to make more? If so, it's probably not a good project to take on. It could be, you know, if it's early on in your business and you're fighting to get started, you're fighting to get a foothold, fighting to get traction, to get positioning. There are times where you do have to take on things that aren't leveraged. Or they might have high strategic value. So there are times where maybe you make a short-term decision to do something that's unleveraged because strategically it will give you leverage in the long run. ## [06:04 - 09:11]

And publishing is one of the best ways to get leverage because it's basically publishing is the idea of taking your thoughts, your ideas, your words, your voice, your writing. And putting it out into the world so that a lot of people can see it. It's the opposite of a one-to-one conversation. One-to-one conversation is fantastic, but you're having it with one person, publishing, you're having it with many people. So this is one of the best ways to get leverage. It's great to get leverage in your marketing. It's great to get leverage in bringing you traffic and putting you in front of a lot of people, running a lot of people through your message. It's a great way to attract prospects. It's great for sales. It's great for product. If you can publish a product, I mentioned this in the last section, my product launch formula. I created a product. I published that product. I can sell it to 10 people or 100 people or 1,000 people or 10,000 people and I don't need to put more effort into it. When I published my book, it took me a long time to write it, but I published it once and now over 100,000 people have read it. That's leverage. Repurposing your whatever you publish. Taking a video and turning it into an audio and turning it into a podcast and turning it into a YouTube video and a Facebook video and then having it transcribed and having it turned into a blog post, that's inherent leverage. That's another form of leverage within your publishing. When you publish, there's lots of different platforms. The platforms keep on expanding. The choice in platforms just keeps on expanding. I'm a huge fan of email. I think email is still king. It's a fantastic, it needs to be part of your publishing. It might not be the only part, but it's the part that you control the most. Other ways to publish, you can might be on social media platforms and they can be great to publish, but you don't own those platforms. What you always want to do is focus on that email list and building that email list and then using that email list to drive people to your whatever you're publishing, however you're publishing, the email is the hub that then sends traffic out into all those areas. The traffic you can control is the inherent traffic that's built in your email list. If you have 10,000 people on your list, at any time you can send an email and tell those 10,000 people to go watch this video, go listen to this podcast, go read this blog post, go look at these photos, whatever. Now, when you push that send button, do 10,000 people go? No, maybe it's only 1,000, but 1,000 people is a lot. It's huge. So email is king and it's sort of the central hub that then sends traffic out in all these different directions.

## [09:11 - 12:12]

Now, then once you get outside of that in those other directions, those other platforms, they all have their strengths and weaknesses and they come and go. If you have an older demographic than Instagram and Snapchat are probably not the places to be, right? Maybe it's Pinterest. If you're really great on video, then you got a few choices. It could be YouTube or it could be Instagram. It could be Facebook. You might be great live on live video or you might be better on more scripted video in a set. So you have to take a look at what your capabilities are, what your interests are, where your market is at and then have that dictate where you want to publish. Because there is no one medium that's right for everyone. Now one great way to add leverage into your business is consider adding in some continuity, some continuity sales. So continuity is a subscription sale. So you make the sale once and people keep on paying you until they cancel. So I've almost always had some form of continuity in my business. When I started out, I had newsletters about the stock market. So people would pay me every month or every year to get access to my newsletters. Now I have high-end coaching programs that renew on a yearly basis. I have my high-end Platinum Plus mastermind group that renews on a yearly basis. Lots and lots of different ways to do continuity. It fits into all different kinds of businesses, of types of products. Nowadays, most of the software you get is on a continuity basis. So continuity is fantastic. It sort of lets you relax because you know how much money is coming in every month. You don't have to go out and make a whole bunch of new sales. You just renew every one once a month or it could be once a year. So it lets you relax. It gives you some level of a guaranteed income. Now it does take work. And if you're the one that, if you're creating content for it, then you will continue to have that work, what I call a mental overhang of having to create that content.

## [12:12 - 15:20]

So don't dismiss that lightly, Beuse. It can be a grind. It can wear you down. But there is forms of continuity where you don't have to do that. If you have a community type of program or in the first section of this training, I talked about this one membership site I had that was just nothing but a user group for a piece of investing software. Then I didn't have, I had zero mental overhang on that. Now that doesn't say that just because you create something where you are creating content every month or every quarter or every year, that doesn't mean it's a horrible thing because it is still highly leveraged and it can scale. It is just something that you have to keep up with. So you have to think that through and think about whether you're willing to do that. Now if you do add continuity, it's great because it does let you relax some. It gives you that guaranteed income. But what you will have is the renewal rate will become one of the most important metrics in your business that you will end up obsessing over. And you're always going to be fighting churn. The churn is this idea of people coming, people go. And there is a little bit, especially if you're creating content, if you're not replacing people as quickly as they're leaving and hopefully more than replacing them and growing, then it becomes psychologically more burdensome to create content if your income is dropping. And another thing is as the program grows, as it scales, then it becomes more difficult to replace the people that leave just on a percentage basis. If you have 100 people and 90% of them renew every month, that means you lose 10 people a month. And so you have to replace 10 people a month to keep your income the same. You have to replace more than 10 people to increase your income. If you have 10,000 people in that program, 10,000 people and you have a 90% renewal, now you're losing 1,000 people every month and now you have to replace 1,000 people. That's a bigger task. So none of this is rocket science, but it's also stuff I never thought about when I first started creating a continuity program, and it's something I want you to think about. ## [15:20 - 18:22]

and keeping my business simple. I can tell you there's a lot of people in my market, a lot of them are good friends of mine who have very similar businesses to mine. And I also have some special insight into the background of most of those businesses, and I'll tell you that most of them are more complex than mine. So it's easy to make things complicated, and you want to avoid it in general. In general, you want to try to keep things as simple as possible and focus on having as high a margin as possible. Now, another thing that I'll give you leverage is having an evergreen marketing system. And I tell you this because I know a lot of the people listening to this will be people that have gone through or are involved in my Product Launch Formula training. And Product Launch Formula has become sort of the facto gold standard for conversions in our market. I mean, it just generates fantastic results. And a lot of people end up building a business purely around episodic launches. And there's an inherent danger in that because sometimes launches don't go fantastic. These things can come up. Hurricanes happen. Terrorists attacks happen. Outages, technical outages happen. Server crashes happen. So there is some risk in just, depending on, say, one launch a year. And creating an evergreen system to continually drive sales is just a way to add in a level of safety into your business. So I would highly suggest looking at creating evergreen systems in your marketing. Another way to get leverage is to reuse your marketing. So this is something I learned early on. I used to back in my stock market days when I was publishing about the stock market, we published every single market day of the year, every single trading day of the year. Most years there's 252 trading days. And I had this system where people could watch a portion of what we were doing on a free trial basis. So they could basically sample our material and it would come out just a little bit late for them. So the utility was greatly diminished, but they could still watch what we were saying was going to happen in the stock market and they could do it every single day. And they could do it as long as they wanted. And one time I sat down to do an analysis of how long people were on my list before they ended up buying.

## [18:23 - 21:16]

And what I noticed is of the people that bought, about half of them were buying the first week. And about another 25% were buying the first 30 days. So 75% of the people that would buy from me would buy within the 30 days. But the other 25% would be spread out over the next several months and even longer. And then I found someone who I identified who had actually been on my email list for more than four years before he purchased. In other words, he'd gotten more than 1,000 emails from me until it was time for him to buy. He was eventually convinced of the value or it was just the right time in his life or it was affordable for him or whatever. I don't know why, but after four years he decided to buy. And that's what drove in. I mean, it was just a huge lesson in this idea of don't get sick of your marketing. Keep your marketing going. And it's only to have marketing stamina. Having the stamina in your marketing to keep on going. Most people get sick of their own marketing before the market does. So have a continual process to just nonstop be marketing to your prospects. And feel free to reuse emails, to reuse marketing campaigns, to reuse webinars, to reuse your launches. Don't get sick of your marketing because in this business, certainly creating product and serving your customers is one of the most important things. It's not a big thing you need to do, but also creating your marketing is a big task. And when you can create marketing that lives on, that you can reuse over and over and over, that's where you get leverage. That's a really big leverage point. It's creating marketing that will continue to work. That is evergreen. That's not a one time deal. It doesn't mean you can't do one time campaigns. That you can't take advantage of something that changes in your market. Of something that, of an event that comes up, of an outside event. You can feel free to take care of those, but always in your marketing ask yourself, how can I create this so this marketing can be used over and over and over. Whether it's videos or sales letters or sales videos or emails or whatever. How can I build this so I can use it over and over and over. So those are some of the keys I'm creating leverage in your business. And in the next section, I'm going to talk about scale and how to scale.
